sources_audited.note: BLS supports the cited occupational duties, CAD/BIM use, code and accessibility considerations, site and contractor work, 2024 employment, 3 percent 2024-2034 employment projection, and renovation/code/accessibility demand factors. It is transparently treated as occupational evidence and as a proxy, not direct industry AI-performance or output evidence.

O*NET supports the cited task inventory, including ADA-compliant planning, CAD construction documents, code research, client consultation, professional coordination, shop-drawing review, site inspection, rendering, materials selection, cost estimation, and client presentation.

The Census NAPCS product list supports the stated NAICS 541410 service scope across residential, nonresidential, hospitality, and retail work, including programming, design development, specifications, construction documents, and construction contract administration. It does not measure eligibility or transferability, as disclosed.

ASID Minnesota supports the cited fixed, hourly, and cost-plus fee methods, their use or combination by most residential and many commercial designers, and the specified-percentage mechanics of cost-plus. It does not establish national fee shares, AI pass-through, or the numeric q estimate, as disclosed.

caveats: The a, rho, and o estimates are inferred from occupational task descriptions rather than measured AI performance, implementation, wage-weighted industry workflows, or future operator-required demand for NAICS 541410.
The e and s5 estimates lack direct evidence on target eligibility, transferability, and five-year qualifying control-transfer incidence; the resulting F factor is suitable for screening only.
The q estimate rests on qualitative Minnesota chapter fee-method guidance that does not establish national revenue shares or realized AI-related price pass-through.
The d5 base uses a ten-year occupational employment projection as a five-year service-demand proxy; employment, industry output, and near-term residential and commercial project cycles can diverge.

Residential, commercial, hospitality, healthcare, and retail work are compressed into one primitive set despite potentially material differences in workflow exposure, regulation, buyer power, and software-only substitution.
summary: Mechanics pass, every score-driving source is reachable and supports its cited factual role, evidence states are honest, and the lens and factor semantics are coherent. The record remains highly assumption-dependent, especially for AI implementation, transfer incidence, retention, and occupational-to-industry demand bridging, but correcting or downgrading the identified weaknesses is not shown to change the base action from VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS or the base tier under the brief's literal materiality test. Publish with the stated caveats.
